column1: # Function definition: the full range of `def NAME …` forms.
# This spec consolidates every shape `def` accepts:
#   1. Simple-value def      — `def NAME 3` — substitutes the value
#                               at every use of NAME.
#   2. List-body splice      — `def NAME [body]` — splices the body's
#                               tokens inline at every use of NAME.
#                               No params, no sandbox.
#   3. Single-sig fn         — `def NAME fn [ [in] [out] [body] ]` —
#                               builds a typed function with one
#                               signature. The outer list is required;
#                               `fn` takes exactly one arg, the list
#                               of triples.
#   4. Multi-sig fn          — `def NAME fn [ [in] [out] [body]
#                                             [in] [out] [body] ... ]`
#                               Same shape as 3 with N triples flat in
#                               the outer list. Each triple becomes an
#                               overload; dispatch picks per call site.
#   5. args frame access     — inside a fn body, `args` returns the
#                               positional arg list pushed by the call
#                               site (in sig order).
#   6. Optional params       — `[a:Integer b:Integer ?]` — a standalone
#                               `?` Word marks the preceding param
#                               optional. Each present/omitted
#                               combination becomes its own overload
#                               with omitted slots filled by the type's
#                               BaseValue.
#   7. Quoted-list def       — `def NAME quote [body]` — the body is
#                               stored as a data list, NOT spliced.
#                               Use lengthq / firstq to inspect it.
# Companion files:
#   numbers.tsv              — arithmetic dispatch with Number contagion.
#   forth.tsv                — defs that build the canonical Forth
#                               combinators on top of dup/swap/over/etc.
#   def-typed.tsv            — typed simple-value defs (`def n:Integer 5`).
# === Test fixtures (defined in aqleng/go/spec_test.go::registerSpecWords) ===
#   addq     — [Number Number] forward-prec — args[1] + args[0] (decimal contagion)
#   concatq  — [String String] forward-prec — args[1] + args[0] (string concat)
#   firstq   — [List] forward-prec — first element or None for empty list
#   lengthq  — [List] forward-prec — list length as Integer
#   mulq     — [Number Number] forward-prec — args[1] * args[0] (decimal contagion)
#   subq     — [Number Number] forward-prec — args[1] - args[0] (decimal contagion)
#   Pre-bound simple-value defs: pi=3 (Integer), tau=6 (Integer), greeting="hello" (String).
# === end fixtures ===
# tokens
# 1. Simple-value def
# Pre-bound from spec_test.go: pi=3, tau=6, greeting="hello".
pi
column3: bare def reference yields its bound value

column1: pi pi
column3: repeated reference yields two copies on the stack

column1: pi addq 1
column3: def value flows into a forward arg slot

column1: 1 addq pi
column3: def value satisfies a stack arg slot

column1: greeting
greeting concatq greeting
tau
pi mulq tau
column3: mulq with both args from defs

column1: def small 7 small
def small 7 small addq small
column3: user def used in both slots

column1: def msg "hi" msg concatq msg
# 2. List-body splice
# `def NAME [body]` registers a binding. Each occurrence of NAME
# REMOVES the word from the token stream and INSERTS the body's
# elements in its place. No new args frame, no sandbox — the body
# runs against the live caller stack.
def doub [ dup addq ] 5 doub
column3: body executes against stack=[5]: dup→[5,5], addq→[10]

column1: def square [ dup mulq ] 7 square
def inc [ 1 addq ] 5 inc
column3: body adds 1 to top of stack

column1: def add5 [ addq 5 ] 10 add5
column3: curried form — body uses an embedded constant

column1: def double-then-inc [ dup addq 1 addq ] 5 double-then-inc
# Splice fires at the use site, NOT at definition time. Insufficient
# stack at the use site is an error — the body sees whatever the
# caller stack happens to be.
def doub [ dup addq ] doub 5
column3: splice fires before 5 is pushed; dup needs 1 stack value, sees empty stack

column1: def doub [ dup addq ] doub
column3: bare invocation; nothing on the stack for dup

column1: # Composition: a body can reference other defs. On splice each name
# spawns its own splice (or, for fn-defs, dispatch).
def doub [ dup addq ] def quad [ doub doub ] 3 quad
def inc [ 1 addq ] def add2 [ inc inc ] 5 add2
# Empty-body splice is a no-op.
def noop [ ] 5 noop
column3: splice removes the word and inserts nothing; 5 stays

column1: def noop [ ] noop
# Parens isolate each invocation so the body's forward-prec ops don't
# greedily grab outside arguments.
def doub [ dup addq ] ( 3 doub ) ( 5 doub )
column3: without the parens addq inside doub would consume the outer 5

column1: # 3. Quoted-list def — body stored as DATA, not spliced
# `quote` flips the list's Quoted flag. The def-stack lookup branch
# in stepWord skips the splice for Quoted lists and treats them as
# values.
def lst quote [ 1 2 3 ] lengthq lst
column3: lst resolves to the quoted list value; lengthq returns its length

column1: def lst quote [ 1 2 3 ] firstq lst
column3: first element of the data list

column1: # 4. Single-sig fn
# `fn [ [in] [out] [body] ]` builds a typed FnDef from a single
# triple. The outer list is required — `fn` takes exactly one arg,
# the list of triples (multi-sig form below extends to N triples).
# Param tokens `name:Type` are split by the fn-param parser; bodies
# reference each name as a bare word that resolves to the matched
# arg via the def stack.
# `def NAME fn [...]` works without paren-wrapping `fn` because
# def's forward marker (insertForward) defers def's match until its
# second slot is filled. fn dispatches first, produces the FnDef,
# and def then fires with [Word(NAME), FnDef].
# Single Integer param.
def double fn [ [ a:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ a a addq ] ] 5 double
def inc fn [ [ n:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n 1 addq ] ] 7 inc
def square fn [ [ x:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ x x mulq ] ] 6 square
# Two Integer params, forward + stack split.
def add-ab fn [ [ a:Integer b:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ a b addq ] ] 3 add-ab 4
def sub-ab fn [ [ a:Integer b:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ subq a b ] ] 10 sub-ab 3
# Three params, all-stack call form.
def trip3 fn [ [ a:Integer b:Integer c:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ a b addq c addq ] ] 1 2 3 trip3
# Zero-arg fn — the body runs with no params; result is the body's
# residual stack.
def zero fn [ [ ] [ Integer ] [ 0 ] ] zero
def fortytwo fn [ [ ] [ Integer ] [ 42 ] ] fortytwo
# Composition: one fn calls another by name.
def double fn [ [ a:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ a a addq ] ] def quad fn [ [ x:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ x double double ] ] 4 quad
# Parens isolate calls — each invocation binds its own params.
def double fn [ [ a:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ a a addq ] ] ( 3 double ) ( 5 double )
column3: without parens the inner addq would greedily consume the outer 5

column1: # 5. Multi-sig fn — N triples in the outer list
# A single outer list contains [in out body in out body …]. Each
# triple becomes one FnSig. Dispatch picks the matching sig at the
# call site by arity (arg count) and type (most-specific first).
# Two arity overloads: 1-arg (double) and 2-arg (multiply).
def f fn [ [ n:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n addq n ] [ n:Integer m:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n m mulq ] ] f 5
def f fn [ [ n:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n addq n ] [ n:Integer m:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n m mulq ] ] f 5 3
# Two type overloads — same arity, different types.
def g fn [ [ n:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n addq n ] [ s:String ] [ String ] [ s concatq s ] ] g 7
def g fn [ [ n:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n addq n ] [ s:String ] [ String ] [ s concatq s ] ] g "hi"
# Three overloads: 0-arg, 1-arg, 2-arg.
def h fn [ [ ] [ Integer ] [ 0 ] [ n:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n ] [ n:Integer m:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n m addq ] ] h
def h fn [ [ ] [ Integer ] [ 0 ] [ n:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n ] [ n:Integer m:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n m addq ] ] h 5
def h fn [ [ ] [ Integer ] [ 0 ] [ n:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n ] [ n:Integer m:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ n m addq ] ] h 5 3
# 7. args frame
# Inside a fn body, `args` returns a list of the matched args in sig
# order. The frame is pushed at call entry and popped when the body
# returns. Outside any fn call, `args` returns the empty list.
args
# Single-arg body uses args lengthq / firstq.
def f1 fn [ [ a:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ args lengthq ] ] 5 f1
def f2 fn [ [ a:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ args firstq ] ] 7 f2
# Two-arg body — args list reflects sig order (sig[0] = forward arg).
def f3 fn [ [ a:Integer b:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ args lengthq ] ] 3 f3 4
def f4 fn [ [ a:Integer b:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ args firstq ] ] 3 f4 4
# Three-arg body, all-stack call.
def f5 fn [ [ a:Integer b:Integer c:Integer ] [ Integer ] [ args lengthq ] ] 1 2 3 f5
# 8. Optional params
# A standalone `?` Word AFTER a param marks the preceding param as
# optional. ExpandOptionalSigs generates one overload per non-empty
# subset of optional params; omitted slots are filled by the type's
# BaseValue (Integer→0, String→"", List→[], etc.). Required params
# (without `?`) are always present. The numbers.tsv §8b block covers
# the numeric BaseValue subset in more depth — these rows pin the
# core mechanism in the function-definition context.
# One required, one optional.
def opt1 fn [ [ a:Integer b:Integer ? ] [ Integer ] [ a b addq ] ] opt1 5
def opt1 fn [ [ a:Integer b:Integer ? ] [ Integer ] [ a b addq ] ] opt1 5 3
# Two optionals — every present/omitted combination dispatches.
def opt2 fn [ [ a:Integer ? b:Integer ? ] [ Integer ] [ a b addq ] ] opt2
def opt2 fn [ [ a:Integer ? b:Integer ? ] [ Integer ] [ a b addq ] ] opt2 5
def opt2 fn [ [ a:Integer ? b:Integer ? ] [ Integer ] [ a b addq ] ] opt2 7 3
# Required arg is still required — calling with too few args errors.
def req fn [ [ a:Integer b:Integer ? ] [ Integer ] [ a b addq ] ] req
